10.1016/j.jmp.2018.04.001
This paper explores the axiomatic foundations of classical representations of choice probabilities. Our primary purpose is to fill a natural position in the gap between the well-known concept of  simple scalability  
                      (Krantz, 1964; Tversky & Russo, 1969)  and its weaker cousin,  monotone scalability  
                      (Fishburn, 1973) . Between these two notions lies a new concept that we call  strict scalability .

The motivation for introducing this new concept is twofold. First, it repairs a “deficiency” in monotone scalability. The latter property is compatible with a utility scale that fails to represent the decision-maker’s stochastic preferences. We say that  
                         a 
                       is “stochastically preferred” to  
                         b 
                       if the decision-maker chooses  
                         a 
                       over  
                         b 
                       more often than she chooses  
                         b 
                       over  
                         a 
                       in a binary choice context. A utility scale “represents” stochastic preferences if it assigns higher utility to  
                         a 
                       than to  
                         b 
                       if and only if (iff)  
                         a 
                       is stochastically preferred to  
                         b 
                      . It is often natural to restrict attention to utility scales with this property. Strict scalability requires monotone scalability  with respect to a utility scale that represents the decision-maker’s stochastic preferences . This is obviously a more stringent requirement than monotone scalability  simpliciter . We show that there exist monotone scalable choice probabilities that are not strictly scalable, and also that strict scalability is strictly weaker than simple scalability.

Second, when alternatives are drawn from a countable set, the strictly scalable binary choice probabilities are precisely those that satisfy the familiar property of  strong stochastic transitivity (SST) , which is equivalent to  weak substitutability  
                      (Davidson & Marschak, 1959) . Strict scalability therefore gives a convenient characterisation of the latter class of binary choice probabilities. Given the prominence of SST and weak substitutability in the literature on probabilistic choice, it is useful to have such a characterisation.

When the set from which alternatives are drawn need not be countable, SST is necessary but not quite sufficient for strict scalability of binary choice probabilities.  Theorem 14  in Section  2.2  provides a set of necessary and sufficient conditions, analogous to those in  Fishburn’s (1973)  representation theorem for monotone scalability ( ibid ., Theorem A).

Section  3  extends our characterisation of strict scalability from binary to multinomial choice probabilities. This complements the work of  Krantz (1964) ,  Smith (1976)  and  Tversky (1972)  on multinomial simple scalability. In order to generalise our representation theorem, we introduce a multinomial generalisation of weak substitutability that we call (not very imaginatively)  multinomial weak substitutability   — see  Definition 25 .

The  Appendix  contains definitions of some standard properties of binary choice probabilities, along with well-known results on simple and monotone scalability in the binary choice context.  Figs. 1 and 2  summarise the relationships discussed in the  Appendix  plus the new results on binary choice established in the present paper. These figures elaborate (parts of) Figure 1 in  Fishburn (1973) , and follow similar principles in their construction — these principles are explained in Section  2.3 .

Given a non-empty set  
                         A 
                       of alternatives, a  complete binary choice specification (CBCS)  gives the probability with which each alternative is chosen from any binary choice set  
                         
                            
                               a 
                               , 
                               b 
                            
                         
                         ⊆ 
                         A 
                      . The “completeness” qualifier emphasises that choice probabilities are defined for  any  doubleton subset of  
                         A 
                      . 
                         1 
                       
                      
                         1 
                         Some authors, such as  Suppes, Krantz, Luce, and Tversky (1989) , use the term “closed” rather than “complete”. 
                      As a formal matter, we also define choice probabilities for “pairs” containing two replicas of the same alternative, but see  Remark 1  on this convention. To avoid non-trivialities, we assume that  
                         A 
                       contains at least two elements.

                            , rather than

In particular, this definition puts our analysis on the same footing as that of  Fishburn (1973)  and  Tversky and Russo (1969)  so our results can be directly compared with theirs. However, this convention does raise some interpretive and technical issues. On the interpretive side, there is the question of whether or not probabilities of the form  
                               P 
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     a

                               2 
                             
                            
                               2 
                               See, for example,  Fishburn (1998, pp.277 and 284) . 
                            ; or else one could assume that there exist two replicas of each  
                               a

                               A 
                            , as in  Smith (1976) , so that subjects can be offered binary choices between two identical alternatives. In the latter case,  (2)  becomes a substantive behavioural assumption, provided one can operationalise the idea of choosing the “first” replica over the “second”. 
                               3 
                             
                            
                               3 
                               Assumption  (2)  is in the spirit of  Yellott’s (1977)  “invariance under uniform expansions of the choice set”. (See also  Krantz, 1964 , p.146.) However, it is not, strictly speaking, implied by Yellott’s principle, which applies only to choice sets with at least two distinct alternatives. 
                            In the former case, one must be careful that assumption  (2)  does not introduce unwarranted restrictions on choice behaviour via axioms imposed on  
                               P 
                             — an issue to which we return in Section  2.4 .

                                     ≿ 
                                  
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                               
                            . Weak substitutability implies the reverse inclusion.  □

Suppose  
                               
                                  
                                     A 
                                     , 
                                     P 
                                  
                               
                             is such that  
                               
                                  
                                     ≿ 
                                  
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                               
                               =

                                     0 
                                  
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                               
                            . Then, for any  
                               a

                               b

                               c 
                               ∈

                            , if  
                               P 
                               
                                  
                                     a

                               ≥

                             (i.e.,  
                               a 
                               
                                  
                                     ≿

                               b 
                            ) it follows that  
                               a

Conversely, suppose  
                               
                                  
                                     A 
                                     , 
                                     P 
                                  
                               
                             satisfies weak substitutability. Setting  
                               c 
                               = 
                               b 
                             in  (6)  gives

                               ⊆ 
                               
                                  
                                     ≿ 
                                  
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                               
                            . Weak substitutability implies the reverse inclusion.  □

Lemma 3  highlights a subtle logical distinction that will be critical to our analysis. The claim that  
                         
                            
                               ≿ 
                            
                            
                               P 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               ≿ 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                            
                               P 
                            
                         
                       is equivalent to the statement that the following holds for all  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                      :  
                         
                            
                               P 
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                  
                               
                               ≥ 
                               
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               
                               
                               
                               ⇔ 
                               
                               
                               
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                     
                                        
                                           a 
                                           , 
                                           c 
                                        
                                     
                                     ≥ 
                                     P 
                                     
                                        
                                           b 
                                           , 
                                           c 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                     
                                     for all  
                                     c 
                                     ∈ 
                                     A 
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      This is a  weaker  claim than the statement that the following holds for all  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         , 
                         c 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                      :  
                         
                            
                               P 
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                  
                               
                               ≥ 
                               
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               
                               
                               
                               ⇔ 
                               
                               
                               
                               P 
                               
                                  
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     c 
                                  
                               
                               ≥ 
                               P 
                               
                                  
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     c 
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      The latter statement asserts that  
                         
                            
                               A 
                               , 
                               P 
                            
                         
                       satisfies  substitutability  ( Definition 37  in the  Appendix ).

Given a CBCS,  
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  P 
                               
                            
                         , it is conventional to consider whether there exists a utility function (or “scale”),  
                            u 
                            
                            : 
                            
                            A 
                            → 
                            R 
                         , such that  
                            P 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                               
                            
                          can be determined using only information on  
                            u 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            u 
                            
                               
                                  b 
                               
                            
                         . In this case, we say that  
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          is “scalable”. Of course, provided the cardinality of  
                            A 
                          is no greater than that of the continuum,  
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          is trivially scalable by assigning a  distinct  utility value  
                            u 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                               
                            
                          to each element of  
                            a 
                            ∈ 
                            A 
                          and defining a function  
                            F 
                          on  
                            u 
                            
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                            × 
                            u 
                            
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                          by specifying  
                            F 
                            
                               
                                  u 
                                  
                                     
                                        a 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  u 
                                  
                                     
                                        b 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                            = 
                            P 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                               
                            
                         . However, such a utility function need not be a “scale” for measuring anything of interest. Ideally, a utility scale should measure the implicit “attractiveness” of a stimulus that is consistently reflected in choice probabilities. The following three, increasingly stringent notions of scalability are therefore natural to consider. (A summary of the key differences follows the formal statements of definitions.)

Definition 4 
                            A CBCS

                                        , 
                                        P

                                        0 
                                        , 
                                        1

                                  F 
                                is non-decreasing (respectively, non-increasing) in its first (respectively, second) argument, and  
                                  
                                     (8) 
                                     
                                        P 
                                        
                                           
                                              a 
                                              , 
                                              b 
                                           
                                        
                                        = 
                                        F 
                                        
                                           
                                              u 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    a 
                                                 
                                              
                                              , 
                                              u 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    b 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               for all  
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A

                                        , 
                                        P

                                        u 
                                        ,

Note that both monotone and strict scalability impose only weak monotonicity restrictions on  
                            F 
                         , while simple scalability requires  
                            F 
                          to be  strictly  monotone in each argument. The essential difference between monotone and strict scalability is that the latter requires  
                            u 
                          to represent stochastic preferences while the former does not. In other words,  
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          is strictly scalable through  
                            
                               
                                  u 
                                  , 
                                  F 
                               
                            
                          iff it is monotone scalable through  
                            
                               
                                  u 
                                  , 
                                  F 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            u 
                          represents (in the usual sense) the binary relation  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          defined by  (4) .

The SS concept is classical in the literature, 
                            5 
                          
                         
                            5 
                            However, the standard definition of SS usually treats probabilities in  
                                  
                                     
                                        0 
                                        , 
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                as special cases, and this standard definition may therefore appear to be “weaker” than ours. In fact,  Definition 6  is equivalent to the standard one — see the discussion following  Theorem 39  in the  Appendix . 
                         and was introduced by  Krantz (1964) . 
                            6 
                          
                         
                            6 
                            In fact,  Krantz (1964)  introduced a more general formulation of simple scalability, suitable for  multinomial  choice. This formulation is discussed in Section  3 . 
                         The MS concept is less well-known but also of long standing — it was introduced by  Fishburn (1973) . The StS concept has not, to the best of our knowledge, appeared previously in the literature. It is immediate that StS and SS are more stringent requirements than MS, and that StS implies MS. That SS implies StS is less obvious.

Lemma 7 
                            
                               If  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               satisfies SS then it satisfies StS, but not conversely.

If  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               satisfies SS then it satisfies StS, but not conversely.

Proof 
                            Suppose  
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                is simply scalable through  
                                  
                                     
                                        u 
                                        , 
                                        F 
                                     
                                  
                               . It suffices to show that  
                                  u 
                                represents  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                               . This may be established as follows: for any  
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                               ,  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           P 
                                           
                                              
                                                 a 
                                                 , 
                                                 b 
                                              
                                           
                                           > 
                                           
                                              
                                                 1 
                                              
                                              
                                                 2 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                           
                                           ⇔ 
                                           
                                           
                                           P 
                                           
                                              
                                                 a 
                                                 , 
                                                 b 
                                              
                                           
                                           > 
                                           P 
                                           
                                              
                                                 b 
                                                 , 
                                                 a 
                                              
                                           
                                           ⇔ 
                                           
                                           
                                           F 
                                           
                                              
                                                 u 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       a 
                                                    
                                                 
                                                 , 
                                                 u 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       b 
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                           > 
                                           F 
                                           
                                              
                                                 u 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       b 
                                                    
                                                 
                                                 , 
                                                 u 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       a 
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                           ⇔ 
                                           
                                           
                                           u

                                           u

                               where the first equivalence uses  (1)  and the final equivalence uses the facts that  
                                  F 
                                is defined on  
                                  u 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                  
                                  × 
                                  u 
                                  
                                     
                                        B 
                                     
                                  
                                , is strictly increasing in its first argument and strictly decreasing in its second. 
                            The following example illustrates a CBCS that is StS but not SS.

Suppose  
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                is simply scalable through  
                                  
                                     
                                        u 
                                        , 
                                        F 
                                     
                                  
                               . It suffices to show that  
                                  u 
                                represents  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                               . This may be established as follows: for any  
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                               ,  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           P 
                                           
                                              
                                                 a 
                                                 , 
                                                 b

The following example illustrates a CBCS that is StS but not SS.

To better understand the difference between monotone and strict scalability, it is useful to observe the following:

Lemma 9 
                            
                               Let  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               be a CBCS that is monotone scalable through  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        u 
                                        , 
                                        F 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               . Then the following holds for any  
                               
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                               
                               :  
                               
                                  
                                     (11) 
                                     
                                        u 
                                        
                                           
                                              a 
                                           
                                        
                                        ≥ 
                                        u 
                                        
                                           
                                              b 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        ⇒ 
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        F 
                                        
                                           
                                              u 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    a 
                                                 
                                              
                                              , 
                                              u 
                                              
                                                 
                                                    b 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        ≥ 
                                        
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                        . 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               The converse implication holds for all  
                               
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                                
                               iff  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               is also strictly scalable through  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        u 
                                        , 
                                        F 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               . In particular, not all monotone scalable CBCSs are strictly scalable.

Let  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P

                                  a

                                        u 
                                        , 
                                        F 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               . In particular, not all monotone scalable CBCSs are strictly scalable.

Proof 
                            If  
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                is monotone scalable through  
                                  
                                     
                                        u 
                                        , 
                                        F

                                     (12) 
                                     
                                        F 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                              , 
                                              y 
                                           
                                        
                                        + 
                                        F 
                                        
                                           
                                              y 
                                              , 
                                              x 
                                           
                                        
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                               for all  
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  u 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                  
                               . Hence,  
                                  F 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                        , 
                                        x 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                for all  
                                  x 
                                  ∈ 
                                  u 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                  
                               . From the monotonicity properties of  
                                  F 
                                it follows that  
                                  F 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                        , 
                                        y 
                                     
                                  
                                  ≥ 
                                  
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                whenever  
                                  x 
                                  ≥ 
                                  y 
                               , which gives the implication  (11) . 
                            If  
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                is also strictly scalable through  
                                  
                                     
                                        u 
                                        ,

                                              x 
                                              , 
                                              y 
                                           
                                        
                                        >

                                           < 
                                           ]

                                              2

                                        x 
                                        > 
                                        
                                           [ 
                                           < 
                                           ] 
                                        
                                        y 
                                     
                                  
                               for any  
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  u 
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                  
                                with  
                                  x 
                                  ≠ 
                                  y 
                               . It follows that,  
                                  F 
                                  
                                     
                                        u

                                  u

                                  a 
                                  ,

                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                               . 
                            The following example (which is similar to one on p.338 of  Fishburn (1973)  exhibits a CBCS that is monotone but not strictly scalable, and for which the converse to  (11)  fails. 
                            
                               
                                  Example 10

                                              π

                                                    x 
                                                    , 
                                                    y 
                                                 
                                              
                                              =

                                                             x 
                                                             − 
                                                             y 
                                                             > 
                                                             1 
                                                          
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          
                                                             
                                                                
                                                                   1 
                                                                
                                                                
                                                                   2

                                                             − 
                                                             1 
                                                             ≤ 
                                                             x

                                                    0 
                                                    , 
                                                    1 
                                                    , 
                                                    2 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                     . In particular,  
                                        F 
                                      is non-decreasing (respectively, non-increasing) in its first (respectively, second) argument since

                                              − 
                                              π 
                                              <

                                                    2 
                                                 
                                              
                                              <

                                        =

                                              b

                                        = 
                                        
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                     .

If  
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        ,

The next subsection provides a characterisation of strict scalability that complements the analogous characterisations of simple and monotone scalability by  Fishburn (1973)  and  Tversky and Russo (1969)  respectively. To keep the present paper self-contained, the  Appendix  contains definitions of all axioms for binary choice probabilities that are mentioned (but not defined) in the main text, as well as the key representation theorems from  Fishburn (1973)  and  Tversky and Russo (1969) .

Tversky and Russo (1969)  show that simple scalability is characterised by the  substitutability  property of binary choice probabilities ( Definition 37 );  Fishburn (1973)  established that monotone scalability is characterised by weak independence ( Definition 40 ) if  
                            A 
                          is countable, and by weak independence together with the existence of a utility representation for  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          in the general (not necessarily countable) case.

We prove here that strict scalability is characterised by weak substitutability when  
                            A 
                          is countable, and by weak substitutability together with the existence of a utility representation for  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          in the general case. These facts are established in  Theorem 14  and  Corollary 15 . Recall that weak substitutability is  strictly  weaker than substitutability and  strictly  stronger than weak independence: see Figure 1 in  Fishburn (1973) .

As is well-known, weak substitutability is equivalent to  strong stochastic transitivity (SST)   — see  Definition 35  in the  Appendix ; another concept that has a long and distinguished heritage in the literature. 
                            8 
                          
                         
                            8 
                            
                               Davidson and Marschak (1959)  restrict attention to CBCS’s with  
                                  0 
                                  < 
                                  P 
                                  
                                     
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        b 
                                     
                                  
                                  < 
                                  1 
                                for all  
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                               , but this restriction plays no role in their proof that weak substitutability is equivalent to SST.

Lemma 11 
                            
                               Davidson & Marschak, 1959 
                            
                            
                               A CBCS satisfies SST iff it satisfies weak substitutability.

A CBCS satisfies SST iff it satisfies weak substitutability.

Using this fact, it is easy to see that weak substitutability is strictly stronger than the  conjunction  of weak independence and weak stochastic transitivity; 
                            9 
                          
                         
                            9 
                            While easy to show, we are not aware of this observation having appeared elsewhere in the literature, so we record it here. 
                         recall that the latter two conditions are logically independent (  Fishburn, 1973 , Figure 1).

Lemma 12 
                            
                               For any CBCS, weak substitutability implies both WST and weak independence, but not conversely (i.e., the conjunction of WST and weak independence does  not  imply weak substitutability).

For any CBCS, weak substitutability implies both WST and weak independence, but not conversely (i.e., the conjunction of WST and weak independence does  not  imply weak substitutability).

Proof 
                            It is well known that weak substitutability implies weak independence (  Fishburn, 1973 , Figure 1), and it is clear from  Lemma 11  that weak substitutability implies WST.                                               ⋅

                                        P 
                                      satisfies weak independence. However, weak substitutability is violated:  
                                        P

                                        =

                                     .

This completes the proof of  Lemma 12 .  □

In other words, weak independence (which characterises monotone scalability when  
                            A 
                          is countable) and the transitivity of  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          do not suffice for strict scalability, even if  
                            A 
                          is countable. This fact clarifies a key part of the logical terrain depicted in  Figs. 1 and 2 .

The following is our main result for binary choice probabilities:

Theorem 14 
                            
                               A CBCS  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               satisfies StS iff it satisfies weak substitutability and there exists a utility representation for  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               .

A CBCS  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               satisfies StS iff it satisfies weak substitutability and there exists a utility representation for  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               .

Given weak substitutability, which implies WST and hence the transitivity of  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                         , the existence of a utility representation for  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          is equivalent to  
                            A 
                          containing a countable  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                         -dense subset (  Krantz, Luce, Suppes, & Tversky, 1971 , Section 2.1), which is trivially satisfied for countable  
                            A 
                         . We therefore have:

Corollary 15 
                            
                               If  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               is a CBCS and  
                               
                                  A 
                                
                               is countable, then the following are equivalent: 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     (i) 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 A 
                                                 , 
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        satisfies StS. 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (ii) 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 A 
                                                 , 
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        satisfies weak substitutability. 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (iii) 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 A 
                                                 , 
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        satisfies SST.

If  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               is a CBCS and  
                               
                                  A 
                                
                               is countable, then the following are equivalent:

(i) 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 A 
                                                 , 
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        satisfies StS. 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (ii) 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 A 
                                                 , 
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        satisfies weak substitutability. 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (iii) 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 A 
                                                 , 
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        satisfies SST.

A 
                                                 , 
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        satisfies StS.

A 
                                                 , 
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        satisfies weak substitutability.

A 
                                                 , 
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        satisfies SST.

Interestingly, if  
                            A 
                          is not countable, then  
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          may satisfy weak substitutability but fail to satisfy even monotone scalability (MS).  Fishburn (1973, p.351) , constructs a CBCS,  
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  P 
                               
                            
                         , for which the binary relation  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          does not possess a utility representation, so  
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          does not satisfy MS. It is straightforward to observe that  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          for Fishburn’s example, from which we deduce that  
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  P 
                               
                            
                          satisfies weak substitutability ( Lemma 3 ). 
                            11 
                          
                         
                            11 
                            Hence, Fishburn’s example also shows that weak substitutability does not suffice for StS (unless  
                                  A 
                                is countable) — a fact that is reflected in  Figs. 1 and 2 . 
                         Since  Example 10  shows that MS does not imply WST, and since weak substitutability implies WST (an immediate corollary of  Lemma 11 ) we have:

Lemma 16 
                            
                               MS does not imply weak stochastic transitivity. Weak substitutability implies MS if  
                               
                                  A 
                                
                               is countable but not in general.

MS does not imply weak stochastic transitivity. Weak substitutability implies MS if  
                               
                                  A 
                                
                               is countable but not in general.

Our main result on binary choice can be viewed from two perspectives. On the one hand, it establishes axiomatic foundations for strict scalability (StS) of CBCSs. Since strict scalability occupies a natural intermediate position between monotone and simple scalability, it is of interest to understand these foundations. On the other hand, when  
                            A 
                          is countable, our result provides a convenient mathematical description of the CBCSs that satisfy strong stochastic transitivity (weak substitutability). This characterisation sheds new light on these familiar axioms.

Along the way we have established a number of relationships amongst properties of CBCSs. These complement the relationships summarised in  Fishburn (1973, Figure 1) . To complete the picture, we give one final result and one final example.

Lemma 17 
                            
                               Any CBCS that satisfies weak stochastic transitivity (WST) and weak independence must also satisfy moderate stochastic transitivity (MST).

Any CBCS that satisfies weak stochastic transitivity (WST) and weak independence must also satisfy moderate stochastic transitivity (MST).

Figs. 1 and 2  summarise the relationships amongst the concepts discussed in the paper.  Fig. 1  indicates relationships that hold for arbitrary  
                            A 
                         ; those in  Fig. 2  are valid if  
                            A 
                          is countable. Both figures use the same conventions as Figure 1 in  Fishburn (1973) . Conditions within a single box are equivalent. If an arrow points in only one direction it means that the converse implication is false. If there is no path joining one box to another (in at least one direction) via a series of arrows, then the two conditions are logically independent.

Boxes are labelled on the right-hand side with the sources that verify the equivalence of the properties within (unless the equivalence is trivial, in which case no label appears). These labels use the abbreviations  DM59  for  Davidson and Marschak (1959) ,  TR69  for  Tversky and Russo (1969)  and  F73  for  Fishburn (1973) . Arrows are labelled on the left with sources that verify the implication (unless the implication is trivial, in which case no label appears) and on the right with references to counter-examples to the converse.

To verify the various logical independencies indicated in  Figs. 1 and 2 , recall the following:

• 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  Example 10  shows that monotone scalability does not imply WST – and hence does not imply any property stronger than WST – even if  
                                     A 
                                   is countable. 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     • 
                                  
                               
                               Unless  
                                     A 
                                   is countable, weak substitutability does not imply monotone scalability ( Lemma 16 ), and therefore nothing weaker than weak substitutability implies monotone scalability. 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     • 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  Example 18  shows that, even if  
                                     A 
                                   is countable, MST does not imply weak independence. 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     • 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  Example 10  shows that, even if  
                                     A 
                                   is countable, weak independence does not imply WST.

Example 10  shows that monotone scalability does not imply WST – and hence does not imply any property stronger than WST – even if  
                                     A 
                                   is countable.

Unless  
                                     A 
                                   is countable, weak substitutability does not imply monotone scalability ( Lemma 16 ), and therefore nothing weaker than weak substitutability implies monotone scalability.

Example 18  shows that, even if  
                                     A 
                                   is countable, MST does not imply weak independence.

Example 10  shows that, even if  
                                     A 
                                   is countable, weak independence does not imply WST.

Before moving on to multinomial choice, let us pause to review the issues raised in  Remark 1  concerning the interpretation of probabilities of the form  
                            P 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  a 
                               
                            
                         .

Suppose one does not wish to ascribe any empirical meaning to  
                            P 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                               
                            
                          when  
                            
                               
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                               
                            
                            ∈
