                            = 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        a 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                  | 
                                  
                                  a 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                               
                            
                         . In other words, suppose that  
                            
                               
                                  D 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  A 
                                  × 
                                  A 
                               
                            
                            ⧵ 
                            
                               
                                  Γ 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                          is the empirically relevant domain for binary choice probabilities. In this case, it would be natural to express all binary choice probabilities as functions with domain  
                            
                               
                                  D 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                          and to re-state our definitions accordingly (i.e., by requiring that all pairs of alternatives appearing in those definitions come from  
                            
                               
                                  D 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                         ). This is analogous to what is typically done in analyses of multinomial choice (see Section  3 ).

The following question now arises: If our notation and definitions are re-specified in this manner, would all results continue to hold? In other words, does the “convention” that  
                            P 
                            
                               
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  a 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                          for all  
                            a 
                            ∈ 
                            A 
                          have a material effect on these results? It would certainly be troubling if it did.

Consider our main result:  Theorem 14 . The “convention” that  
                            P

                            =

                                  2 
                               
                            
                         
 for all  
                            a 
                            ∈ 
                            A 
                          does play a role here; it cannot be dispensed with outright. Its role can, however, be played equally well if we augment the definition of strict scalability with the “convention” that  
                            
                               (14) 
                               
                                  F 
                                  
                                     
                                        x

Krantz’s (1964)  original formulation of simple scalability is more general than  Definition 6  since it is not restricted to binary choices. 
                         13

                         A

                         A 
                      , finite or otherwise. After reviewing Tversky’s result, we develop an analogous representation result for a multinomial generalisation of strict scalability.

Tversky’s (1972)  stated definition of simple scalability actually coincides with the (weaker) notion of weak scalability. Subsequent authors (e.g.,  Suppes et al., 1989 ) have amended Tversky’s definition in accordance with the one given above.

Tversky (1972)  characterises multinomial simple scalability using the following multinomial extension of independence ( Definition 38 ):

Proof 
                            If  
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                satisfies order independence, then  Tversky (1972, p. 343) , proves that the first equivalence holds and the second is immediate. The “only if” part of the claim is trivial.  □

If  
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                satisfies order independence, then  Tversky (1972, p. 343) , proves that the first equivalence holds and the second is immediate. The “only if” part of the claim is trivial.  □

Theorem 22 
                            
                               Tversky, 1972 , Corrected 
                            
                               A CFCS satisfies simple scalability iff it satisfies order independence.

A CFCS satisfies simple scalability iff it satisfies order independence.

The implicit “correction” refers to the fact that the result is true only if simple scalability is defined as in  Definition 19 , not as  Tversky (1972)  originally defined it — see  Suppes et al. (1989, Section 17.4.1) .

We wish to encode the principle behind condition  (18)  in a more compact form, and preferably one that more closely resembles Tversky’s order independence.

Definition 25

                                        ,

A CFCS  
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                satisfies  multinomial weak substitutability (MWS)  iff the following holds for all  
                                  B

                                  a

From  Lemma 21  it is obvious that order independence implies MWS. We will show that MWS is equivalent to  (18) .

In preparation, we make the following observations:

                               . The converse is false.

                                  ⊆

                                  ⧵ 
                                  
                                     
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        b 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               . The converse is false.

From our analysis of the binary case (and  Lemma 26 ), we know that the converse to  Lemma 28  cannot hold. However, it does when  
                            A 
                          is countable, as we will shortly see.

With these preliminaries in hand, we may now prove the following Theorem, from which our main representation results follow as immediate corollaries.

Corollary 30

                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               be a CFCS. Then

                               is strictly scalable iff it satisfies MWS and there exists a utility representation for  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               .

                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P

As previously observed, the existence of a utility representation for

                          is equivalent to  
                            A

                            A

                                  A 
                                
                               countable. Then  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               is strictly scalable iff it satisfies MWS.

Let  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A 
                                        , 
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               be a CFCS with  
                               
                                  A 
                                
                               countable. Then  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        A

                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               is strictly scalable iff it satisfies MWS.

We have shown that MWS ( Definition 25 ) is a multinomial generalisation of weak substitutability that allows us to extend our characterisation of strict scalability from the binary to the multinomial choice setting.

Since strict scalability is weaker than simple scalability, it is useful to understand the former’s relationship with  Smith’s (1976)  notion of weak scalability, which is also weaker than simple scalability. In fact, the two concepts are logically independent. The example in  Smith (1976, Section 4)  satisfies weak scalability but is easily seen to violate strict scalability. To see that strictly scalability does not imply weak scalability, consider the following example.

Example 32

While the concepts are familiar, the reader is reminded that terminology does vary across the literature. We use “strict stochastic transitivity” in the sense of  Fishburn (1973) .  Tversky and Russo (1969) , on the other hand, use the term “strong stochastic transitivity” to refer to what we have labelled StST. Most authors use “strong stochastic transitivity” for the property in  Definition 35  so we have adopted the more conventional usage.

Tversky and Russo (1969)  prove that a CBCS is simply scalable iff it satisfies StST, and that the latter is equivalent to each of the following properties:

Definition 37 
                         A CBCS satisfies  substitutability  iff the following holds for any

                                           a 
                                           , 
                                           c 
                                        
                                     
                                     ≥ 
                                     P 
                                     
                                        
                                           b 
                                           , 
                                           c 
                                        
                                     
                                     .

Theorem 39 
                         
                            Tversky & Russo, 1969 
                         
                         
                            Given a CBCS,  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     A 
                                     , 
                                     P

                                              , 
                                              P

                                              A 
                                              , 
                                              P 
                                           
                                        
                                      
                                     satisfies independence.

Weak independence is necessary, but not quite sufficient, for monotone scalability (MS).

Theorem 41 
                         
                            Fishburn, 1973 
                         
                         
                            A CBCS satisfies MS iff it satisfies weak independence and there exists a utility representation for  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ≿ 
                                  
                                  
                                     0 
                                  
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                               
                            
                            . There exists a CBCS that satisfies weak independence but not MS.

                                     ≿

Fishburn (1973, Theorem 1)  shows that weak independence is satisfied iff  
                         
                            
                               ≿ 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                            
                               P 
                            
                         
                       is a weak order. Therefore, if  
                         
                            
                               A 
                               , 
                               P 
                            
                         
                       is a CBCS that satisfies weak independence, there exists a utility representation for  
                         
                            
                               ≿ 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                            
                               P 
                            
                         
                       iff  
                         A 
                       contains a countable  
                         
                            
                               ≿ 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                            
                               P 
                            
                         
                       -dense subset ( ibid ., pp. 350–351). When  
                         A 
                       itself is countable, this latter condition is trivially satisfied.
